title: John Holland Bridges (1814-1857): From the Sheffield Intervales to the Aroostook Frontier
narrative: John Holland Bridges was born on 28 April 1814 at Sheffield, in Sunbury County, New Brunswick, on the east bank of the Saint John River. Sheffield Parish was one of the original parishes of Sunbury County, first settled in the 1760s by New England Planters (Puritan migrants from Massachusetts) before being augmented by Loyalist refugees after the American Revolution; the central settlement grew out of the older township of Maugerville and produced the province's first Protestant church in 1775. The lower Saint John intervales around Sheffield, Maugerville and Gagetown were prized for their rich flood-plain hay and farmland, and it was into this established river-valley farming community that John was born during the closing years of the War of 1812.

His parents were Samuel Bridges Jr. and Mary 'Polly' Robinson Taylor. Genealogical compilations of the family record that Samuel married Mary Robinson Taylor (called 'Polly'), a daughter of James Taylor, on 31 January 1805; Mary was born in 1786 and is reported to have died in 1862 and to be buried at Jacksontown, in Carleton County, New Brunswick. The Bridges family was long established at Sheffield, where Samuel and other forebears are recorded as yeomen of the parish. John was thus one of a large family of New Brunswick-born Bridges children, several of whom (including siblings recorded as born at Sheffield in the 1820s) would, like John, eventually drift westward and southward toward the Maine border country.

John Holland Bridges came of age in the borderland whose ownership was still openly disputed. The boundary between New Brunswick and the State of Maine had never been clearly fixed by the 1783 Treaty of Paris, and through the 1830s rival American and New Brunswick lumbermen cut timber in the contested Aroostook valley. The tension culminated in the bloodless 'Aroostook War' of 1838-1839, when Maine and New Brunswick called out their militias and General Winfield Scott brokered a truce without a battle being fought; the line was finally settled by the Webster-Ashburton Treaty of 1842, which awarded most of the disputed territory, including the Aroostook and the Mars Hill area, to the United States. It was across precisely this newly fixed border that John and others of the Bridges connection moved, joining a stream of New Brunswick families settling the Aroostook frontier in the 1840s and 1850s.

Local Aroostook tradition places John Holland Bridges among the earliest settlers of the Mars Hill district. Town and county histories record that the first settler, Moses Snow, arrived in 1844, followed by John Ruggles in 1847; and that 'Holland Bridges' (the same man entered in census records as John H. Bridges) 'built the first frame house in the present town' of Mars Hill. He is further remembered as having kept a tavern in the adjoining settlement of Westfield, and a road known later as the Ellis Tote Road or Fort Road ran out from 'Bridges' place,' which his son Reuben helped him grub out in 1856. Notably, the same neighborhood drew his second wife's family: histories of the area state that James Thorncroft built a home in what is now Westfield about 1841, anchoring the Thorncroft name in the same borderland where the Bridges family settled.

John Holland Bridges is credited with two marriages, to Sarah Barker and to Sarah F. Thorncroft, but the chronology of the two unions is not entirely clear in the surviving free sources, and this point should be treated with caution. One marriage record reports that John married Sarah F. Thorncroft on 22 January 1837 (a ceremony placed at Houlton, Maine), with Sarah said to have been born about 1816-1818 in New Brunswick and to have died in 1889. Because his son Joseph Reuben Bridges was born in November 1837, that timing is consistent with Joseph Reuben being a child of Sarah Thorncroft. The relationship to a wife surnamed Barker is harder to pin down: family compilations are inconsistent, with the given name sometimes rendered as 'Sarah' and sometimes as 'Jane' Barker, and the order of the marriages and the division of the children between the two wives cannot be stated with confidence from the free record alone. What is reasonably documented is that John fathered a sizeable family spanning the New Brunswick and Maine years.

Among John's children, his son Joseph Reuben Bridges (9 November 1837 - 14 August 1913) carried the family line forward at Mars Hill. Joseph Reuben, who as a young man helped his father clear the Fort Road in 1856, is buried in Pierce Cemetery at Mars Hill; through his daughter Gertrude Bridges, who married Otis Danforth, the family connects to later generations. By the 1850 federal census the household was enumerated at Mars Hill, Aroostook County, Maine, confirming that the family had fully crossed from the Saint John valley into the American Aroostook by mid-century.

John Holland Bridges died on 5 September 1857 at Mars Hill, Aroostook County, Maine, at the age of only 43. He did not live to see Mars Hill organized as a plantation in 1866 or incorporated as a town in 1867, dying instead in its raw frontier phase, only a year after helping cut the tote road out from his own homestead. His short life traces in miniature the larger story of the New Brunswick-to-Aroostook migration: born on the long-settled Saint John River intervales of Sheffield, he came of age amid an international boundary quarrel, and ended his days as a pioneer builder of the first frame house in a Maine border town that was still, at his death, scarcely more than a clearing in the woods.
